topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eliza stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat. The numbers swam before her eyes, a dizzying cascade of figures that made her stomach clench. She had been meticulously tracking her own quarterly earnings, a small, internal victory she’d been savoring. Then, a routine cross-reference brought up the junior analyst's compensation package. A slow, creeping realization began to spread through her chest, tightening like a vice. She blinked hard, trying to re-read the data, but the discrepancy remained glaringly obvious. When Mark finally approached her desk, his usual breezy demeanor replaced by a strained neutrality, Eliza could only manage a weak nod, her hands suddenly feeling clammy. "Just... reviewing some figures, Eliza," he mumbled, avoiding her gaze. The silence in the office felt vast, amplifying the sudden, jarring reality of the situation.

[story 2]

I felt the blood drain from my face as the spreadsheet updated. It was a slow, sickening drop, like sinking into cold water. My fingers froze over the keyboard, the keys suddenly feeling alien and unresponsive. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and forced myself to look at the screen again. The contrast between my projected salary and the actual figure for Sarah, the new hire, was stark and undeniable. A strange, hollow sensation bloomed in my chest, a sudden lightness followed by a heavy weight settling in my gut. When Sarah walked by, offering a bright, oblivious smile, I could only manage a jerky nod, my voice coming out thin and reedy. "Oh, hi," I managed, the words tasting like ash.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug suddenly felt scalding hot against my palms. I kept glancing from the financial report to the reflection in the darkened monitor, trying to reconcile the visual information with the reality of the figures. It wasn't just a difference; it was a chasm. A tightness built up behind my sternum, a pressure that made it difficult to draw a full breath. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of the wheels against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet cubicle farm. When David finally entered, carrying a stack of files, I stood up too quickly, nearly knocking over my water bottle. "Everything alright, Eliza?" he asked, his tone laced with a careful, probing concern that made my skin crawl. I just shook my head, unable to articulate the sudden, profound imbalance I was experiencing.
